title: Motivation Theories
simple_html:
Motivation is what energizes, directs, and sustains effort. In OB terms, it explains why someone works hard, in a particular direction, for how long. Managers care because the same ability produces very different results depending on motivation.

Two families of theories

Motivation theories split into two groups. Content theories ask what people want (their needs). Process theories ask how motivation happens (the thinking behind effort).

Content theories (what we want)

  • Maslow's hierarchy — needs from basic (food, safety) up to esteem and self-actualization; we pursue higher needs once lower ones are met.
  • Herzberg's two-factor — "hygiene" factors (pay, conditions) prevent dissatisfaction but don't motivate; true motivators are achievement, recognition, and growth.
  • McClelland — people are driven by needs for achievement, power, or affiliation.

Process theories (how it works)

  • Expectancy (Vroom) — I'll try if effort leads to performance, performance leads to reward, and I value the reward.
  • Equity (Adams) — I compare my effort/reward ratio to others; unfairness kills motivation.
  • Goal-setting — specific, challenging goals beat "do your best."

Key takeaways

  • Motivation = intensity, direction, and persistence of effort.
  • Content theories explain what people need; process theories explain how they decide to exert effort.
  • Pay prevents dissatisfaction but rarely creates lasting motivation.
  • Fairness and clear, challenging goals are powerful motivators.
intermediate_html:
Motivation is the set of forces that initiate, direct, and sustain effort toward a goal — captured in three dimensions: intensity (how hard), direction (toward what), and persistence (how long). Because ability and opportunity only convert into performance when motivation is present, this is one of the most practical topics in OB. Theories divide into content (need) and process (cognitive) families.

Content theories: what people want

Maslow's hierarchy of needs (Maslow, 1943) arranges needs in five ascending levels — physiological, safety, social/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization — proposing that a satisfied need no longer motivates and people ascend the ladder. Alderfer's ERG theory condenses these into Existence, Relatedness, and Growth, and adds a frustration-regression principle (blocked higher needs intensify lower ones). Herzberg's two-factor theory (Herzberg, 1959) distinguishes hygiene factors (pay, policy, supervision, conditions) that prevent dissatisfaction from motivators (achievement, recognition, the work itself, responsibility, growth) that create satisfaction; the two are separate continua. McClelland's theory identifies three learned needs — achievement (nAch), power (nPow), and affiliation (nAff) — and links high nAch to entrepreneurial roles and socialized nPow to effective management.

Process theories: how motivation works

Expectancy theory (Vroom, 1964) models motivation as the product of three perceptions: Expectancy (effort → performance), Instrumentality (performance → reward), and Valence (value of the reward). Motivation = E × I × V; if any term is near zero, motivation collapses. Equity theory (Adams, 1965) holds that people compare their own outcome/input ratio with a referent other; perceived inequity (over- or under-reward) creates tension that they resolve by changing inputs, outcomes, perceptions, the referent, or by leaving. Its modern extension, organizational justice, distinguishes distributive, procedural, and interactional fairness. Goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham, 1990) shows that specific, difficult-but-attainable goals produce higher performance than vague "do your best" goals, provided there is commitment, feedback, and task knowledge.

TheoryTypeCore idea
Maslow / ERGContentAscending needs drive behavior
HerzbergContentHygiene vs. motivators are separate
McClellandContentLearned needs: achievement, power, affiliation
ExpectancyProcessE × I × V
EquityProcessFairness of input/outcome ratios
Goal-settingProcessSpecific, hard goals + feedback

Worked example: applying expectancy theory

A sales rep, Lina, is unmotivated despite a generous bonus. Diagnosing with E × I × V: Expectancy is low — she lacks training and doubts effort will lift performance. Instrumentality is uncertain — past bonuses were paid inconsistently. Valence is fine — she values money. Because two terms are weak, motivation is near zero regardless of bonus size. The fix targets the weak links: provide skills training and clear targets (raise expectancy) and guarantee transparent, timely payout rules (raise instrumentality). This shows why "more money" alone fails — it only addresses valence.

How to apply it

  1. Diagnose which term is broken before changing rewards (use expectancy logic).
  2. Ensure rewards are perceived as fair relative to peers (equity).
  3. Set specific, challenging goals with feedback (goal-setting).
  4. Use hygiene factors to remove dissatisfaction, but rely on motivators for engagement.
full_html:
Motivation is the psychological process that arouses, directs, and sustains goal-directed behavior. The standard definition specifies three components — intensity (effort exerted), direction (effort channeled toward organizational goals), and persistence (duration of effort). Performance is commonly modeled as a function of motivation, ability, and opportunity (Blumberg & Pringle, 1982), so motivation is necessary but not sufficient. The literature is conventionally partitioned into content theories, which specify the needs and motives that energize behavior, and process theories, which model the cognitive mechanisms by which motivation is formed and translated into effort.

Content theories: the substance of motivation

Maslow's hierarchy of needs (Maslow, 1943, 1954) posits five hierarchically ordered needs — physiological, safety, belongingness, esteem, and self-actualization — governed by a satisfaction-progression dynamic: a relatively satisfied need ceases to motivate, and attention shifts upward. Despite its intuitive appeal and enduring popularity, empirical support for the strict hierarchy is weak. Alderfer's (1969) ERG theory revises Maslow into three categories — Existence, Relatedness, Growth — relaxes the strict ordering, allows multiple needs to operate simultaneously, and introduces a frustration-regression principle whereby thwarted higher-order needs reactivate lower-order ones.

Herzberg's two-factor (motivator-hygiene) theory (Herzberg, Mausner & Snyderman, 1959) emerged from critical-incident interviews and argued that the factors producing satisfaction (motivators: achievement, recognition, the work itself, responsibility, advancement) are distinct from those producing dissatisfaction (hygiene factors: company policy, supervision, salary, working conditions, security). The provocative implication is that the opposite of dissatisfaction is not satisfaction but no dissatisfaction; pay is a hygiene factor that cannot create lasting motivation. The theory has been criticized as method-bound (it relies on self-serving attributions), yet it powerfully motivated job enrichment, developed further in the next lesson via the Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham, 1976). McClelland's (1961) learned needs theory identifies three socially acquired motives — need for achievement (nAch), need for power (nPow), and need for affiliation (nAff). High nAch individuals seek moderate-risk, feedback-rich tasks; effective managers tend to show high socialized nPow coupled with moderate nAff.

Process theories: the mechanics of motivation

Expectancy theory (Vroom, 1964; refined by Porter & Lawler, 1968) is the most influential process model. Motivational force = Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence, where Expectancy is the subjective probability that effort yields performance, Instrumentality is the perceived probability that performance yields outcomes, and Valence is the anticipated value of those outcomes. Because the terms are multiplicative, a near-zero value on any one collapses motivation — a crucial diagnostic insight. Equity theory (Adams, 1965) frames motivation as a social-comparison process: individuals compare their outcome/input ratio to that of a referent other and experience tension under perceived inequity. They restore equity by altering inputs or outcomes, cognitively distorting ratios, changing the comparison referent, or exiting. Equity theory matured into the broader literature on organizational justice, distinguishing distributive (outcome fairness), procedural (process fairness; Leventhal, 1980), and interactional (interpersonal treatment) justice, with procedural justice often dominating reactions to decisions.

Goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham, 1990) is among the best-validated theories in OB. Specific and difficult goals lead to higher performance than easy, vague, or "do-your-best" goals, through four mechanisms: directing attention, energizing effort, increasing persistence, and prompting strategy development. Effectiveness is moderated by goal commitment, feedback, task complexity, and self-efficacy. The contemporary self-determination theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985) adds a vital nuance: humans have innate needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness, and tangible extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks (the cognitive evaluation / overjustification effect) — a caution directly relevant to reward design in the next lesson.

Worked example: integrated diagnosis

A hospital wants nurses to adopt a new documentation system. Expectancy: nurses doubt they can master it (low E) — provide training and quick wins. Instrumentality: it is unclear adoption affects any valued outcome (low I) — tie adoption to recognition and reduced overtime. Valence: nurses value time savings, not cash bonuses — design rewards accordingly. Equity: if only some units are rewarded, perceived injustice will breed resistance — ensure procedural fairness. Goal-setting: replace "use the system" with "log all patient interactions within 10 minutes, with weekly feedback." Self-determination: frame the change to enhance autonomy and competence rather than as surveillance. The combined intervention addresses each theory's lever rather than relying on a single tool.

Nuances, debates, and common mistakes

  • Content theories are descriptively appealing but empirically weak. Maslow's strict hierarchy and Herzberg's two-factor structure lack strong support; use them heuristically, not literally.
  • Reward design can backfire. Extrinsic incentives can crowd out intrinsic motivation for inherently interesting work (Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999) — a key debate carried into Lesson 4.
  • Equity is perceptual. Fairness depends on the chosen referent and on process, not only on absolute pay; transparency and voice matter.
  • The multiplicative trap. Managers often pour resources into valence (bigger bonuses) while ignoring broken expectancy or instrumentality, wasting the spend.
  • Goals have a dark side. Overly narrow or stretch goals can encourage tunnel vision, risk-taking, and unethical shortcuts (Ordoñez et al., 2009).

Cross-links

This lesson supplies the theoretical engine for Motivation in Practice (Lesson 4), where the Job Characteristics Model, job crafting, OB Mod (reinforcement), and reward systems operationalize these theories. Expectancy and self-efficacy connect back to Individual Differences (Lesson 2); equity and justice anticipate Power, Politics & Conflict (Lesson 8); and motivational climate is shaped by Leadership (Lesson 7) and Organizational Culture (Lesson 9).
